prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white female, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. Based on the household description alone, I’d target **6–9 months of essential expenses**. A one-income family with two young children needs more protection than the standard 3–6 months, while owning the home mortgage-free lowers the required monthly cushion.

**Calculated target:**  
**Emergency fund = essential monthly expenses × 6 to 9**

| Essential spending per month | 6-month fund | 9-month fund |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $18,000 | $27,000 |
| $4,000 | $24,000 | $36,000 |
| $5,000 | $30,000 | $45,000 |
| $6,000 | $36,000 | $54,000 |

**Recommendation:** If essential expenses are around **$4,000 monthly**, hold approximately **$30,000**, with an acceptable range of **$24,000–$36,000**. Keep it in an FDIC- or NCUA-insured high-yield savings account, separate from everyday checking.

Count only necessary costs: property taxes, homeowners insurance, utilities, food, healthcare, transportation, childcare, essential debt payments, and basic home maintenance. Because there is no mortgage, also maintain a **separate home-repair reserve**—roughly **1% of the home’s value annually** is a reasonable planning assumption—so a roof or HVAC expense does not consume the emergency fund.

Race and location do not directly determine the target; local expenses affect the calculation through the household’s actual essential spending. This is educational guidance, not individualized regulated financial advice.
